AN ACCOUNTING MAGAZINE FOR STUDENTS. OFFICE EMPLOYES. 
ACCOUNTANTS AND BUSINESS EXECUTIVES GENERALLY.

By W. S. Zimmerman
A
T LEAST once to every man 
comes a time when he is made 
the boss of others. Some men fumble 
this opportunity; others apparently 
with no more ability make it a step­
ping stone to a successful career.

What is bossing? Why is one man 
able to transform a sluggish group 
of workers into spirited enthusiasts, 
and why does another man given the 
same chance have to be removed?

The first man, we have all seen his 
kind, apparently does nothing all day 
long except to nod a nonchalant “yes” 
or “no” to problems brought to him 
for a decision. The second man 
works furiously, stays long hours, 
keeps in close touch with the work of 
his department, but in spite of drudg­
ery he fails. It doesn’t seem fair.

Bossing, first of all, must conform 
to human nature. As it fails to touch 
off the instinct of loyalty, waken the 
spirit of romance, adventure, or 
sportsmanship, so bossing falls short 
of its purpose.

Modern Pioneers
We are not so very far to-day from 
the times of the great explorers, pio­
neers, and fighters on land and sea. 
These great captains and their lieu­
tenants developed in mankind the in­
stinct for following a gallant leader.

The world is safe for democracy 
these days, but the instinct for fol­

lowing the leader, bred into us for 
centuries, still persists. It shows it­
self now-a-days largely in games 
which the former fighter-explorer 
type plays, or wants to play.

But there is another outlet for the 
instinct for adventure, romance, fol­
low-the-leader, or whatever you 
choose to call it. Business! Business 
has opened a new field for adventure 
and offers all the opportunity for per­
sonal exploits that old-time adventure 
ever did.

The Three in a Big Deal
Some people instinctively sense this 
opportunity. Many men get as much 
satisfaction from closing a big deal 
as a fighter in the good old days got 
from laying out a dozen men on a 
battlefield. To them business is a 
glorious adventure crammed with per­
sonal enjoyment.

But to thousands of other men— 
the big majority, business is drudg­
ery which must be endured. Through 
ignorance, or lacking imagination, 
they fritter away their instinct for 
personal accomplishment.

This is not entirely their fault. For 
their so-called leaders instead of de­
veloping a crew of personal follow­
ers, as in old times, permit themselves 
to get so wrapped up in their per­
sonal affairs, they overlook the greater 
results that would happen by rous-

ing an “up-boys-and-at-’em” spirit 
among their grudging helpers.

Once in a while we hear of men 
who have not overlooked this bigger 
purpose. Charles M. Schwab, presi­
dent of the United States Steel Cor­
poration, is one of them. The story 
of his rise scintillates with tales of 
his purposeful inspiring of men 
through their sporting instincts, their 
natural desire to outdo their fellows, 
their desire for personal accomplish­
ment. To illustrate:

On a tour of his mills one day, 
Schwab asked a furnace man how 
many tons of steel his gang had 
melted that day. On learning that 
it was nine tons he wrote a large fig­
ure “9” on the furnace door. The 
night gang saw the figure, inquired 
about it, and promptly “got a hump 
on.” The day gang on returning to 
work found the “9” erased and a “10” 
in its place. In turn they speeded 
up. The spirit was communicated to 
other melting gangs, and the output of 
each furnace was increased from the 
old average of nine to a new average 
of fourteen tons a shift.

But we do not have to go to 
Schwab or to other great leaders for 
proof of what this play spirit will 
do. Production chiefs, sales manag­
ers, office men all over the country 
have tested the sportive spirit of their 
helpers and found that an appeal to 
it gets results. There is no secret 
about it. Anyone can do it. The 
only wonder is that so few try this 
elementary, basic, but sure-fire 
method.

The “show-off spirit” is so strong 
in humanity it manifests itself in the 
cradle. In irrepressible childhood it 
is plain to the most casual observer. 
Little girls love to dress up in 
mother’s clothes and parade the street. 
Boys enjoy marbles, run races, play

games to prove their superiority over 
their fellows.

But there seems to exist by com­
mon consent a vast conspiracy among 
adults to repress this deep-rooted de­
sire for self-expression. By some 
strange malice the pack of us unite 
to pull down the man who climbs, 
or who tries to climb, above his fel­
lows. The weak man or woman suc­
cumbs and stays on the dead level 
of monotony, but his desire to show- 
off still remains to burst out in all 
kinds of wasteful ways.

Occasionally a real leader occurs. 
He taps this fount of human power 
and by force of it is carried to the 
heights.

Such men are surrounded by loyal, 
enthusiastic, indomitable helpers 
whose released powers are responsible 
for many of the sensational successes 
of American business.

We shall illustrate by just one re­
cent example. In 1911 the Philadel­
phia Rapid Traction Company (the 
street car lines) had spent $30,000,000 
capital and was near bankruptcy. Its 
cars were 95 per cent obsolete, its 
power houses inadequate, its roadbeds 
junk, its men discouraged and 
ashamed of their jobs.

One man, Thomas E. Mitten, who 
then became president, in eleven years 
changed an annual deficit of 
$1,000,000 to annual net earnings of 
$3,000,000, cut down costs, reduced 
accidents, modernized equipment, in­
creased pay, and when a fight for con­
trol of the improved system came up, 
the employees, through their associa­
tion, bought 10,000 shares of stock 
which was voted for Mitten.

That is what leadership will do. 
The result, one of thousands not so 
great, goes back to the first principle 
of bossing, inspiring men. This con­
sists of giving proper vent to the de­
sire “to do” which exists in every 
one of us.
Four

Three New C. P. A.’s
Three students of the I. A. S. 
course have been added to the rap­
idly growing list of those who have 
passed the C. P. A. examinations and 
obtained their degrees.

E. E. Fowler, 1083 Lakepoint Ave­
nue, Grosse Point Park, Mich., was 
the successful candidate in his state. 
In his letter of December 30 he ac­
knowledges that I. A. S. training was 
largely instrumental in helping him 
to pass.

No doubt you will be interested in 
learning of my success in the recent 
Certified Public Accountant examination. 
I have received my certificate and con­
sider it, primarily, the result of my 
training with your school.

I entered the examination room with 
a feeling of full confidence that I would 
be successful. I felt that with my I. 
A. S. training (especially Mr. Sunley’s 
C. P. A. Coaching) I was master of 
the situation. With the exception of 
Practical Accounting I finished all sub­
jects before the allotted time; and, while 
the examination was far from being 
“easy,” I found no “stumbling blocks” 
that I could not master.

I wish the International Accountants 
Society all the success possible in their 
constructive work, and you may rely on 
me to recommend it at every oppor­
tunity.

L. T. Garrison, 3313 “N” Street, 
N. W., Washington, D. C., passed the 
North Carolina examination. He 
wrote:

It is impossible for me to tell you how 
much your course helped me.

Earl Puckett, 109 St. Clair Street, 
Indianapolis, was the successful can­
didate in Indiana. He has been em­
ployed in his home city by a well- 
known firm of Certified Public Ac­
countants.

The longer I live, the more I am 
certain that the great difference be­
tween men—between the feeble and 
the powerful, the great and the insig­
nificant—is earnestness, invincible de­
termination ; a purpose once fixed, 
and then, death or victory!—Buxton.

The Railroad Situation
An era of prosperity for the rail­
roads in 1923 was predicted by Julius 
H. Parmelee, director of the Bureau 
of Railway Economics. Net operat­
ing incomes increased $145,000,000 
during 1922, Mr. Parmelee said, and 
represented a return of 4.05 per cent 
on the valuation fixed by the Inter­
state Commerce Commission. In 1921 
the rate was 3.3 per cent, while in 
the previous year it was a small frac­
tion of 1 per cent. Gross returns 
were greater than in 1921 by $30,000,- 
000, while operating expenses have 
been reduced nearly $140,000,000. 
Taxes were the heaviest on record, be­
ing $20,000,000 greater than in 1921, 
and reaching nearly $300,000,000.

Railroad officials, on the Other 
hand, are less optimistic and are con­
cerned more over the ability of the 
roads to cope with the expected in­
crease in traffic. “Either the trans­
portation companies are going to be 
permitted to earn a fair return and 
give adequate service, or American 
business and agriculture, in insisting 
upon lower rates, must be willing to 
accept a service which will by ho 
means meet their demands,” is the 
way they put it.—Forbes.

Savings Accounts
A recent compilation by this divi­
sion showed for the first time that 
there are more savings accounts in 
America than there are families and 
that one-half of all bank deposits and 
one-third of all bank resources are in 
the savings accounts. There is no 
way of estimating the number of 
owners of war savings certificates, 
but we presume that it is so large as 
to add materially to the number of 
actual savers with which this coun­
try should be credited.—Samuel H. 
Beach, president of the Savings Bank 
Division of the American Bankers' 
Association.

Dawes’ report shows department not self-sustaining and reveals huge 
annual deficit.

Ordinarily balance sheets are 
prosaic reading, but the report for 
the post office department on the 
first quarter of 1922 which had been 
suppressed, was a first-page news 
story on the morning of December 
22. The following is a reprint from 
The Chicago Tribune.

GEN. CHARLES G. DAWES has 
made public the suppressed bal­
ance sheet of the post office depart­
ment of the United States Govern­
ment.

“For the first time in the history 
of this country,” the former director 
of the budget asserted, “there was 
prepared not only a balance sheet of 
the post office department in terms 
understandable by the average man, 
but a report in the accepted business 
form of the current operations of the 
department for the first quarter of 
this year.”

Huge 1922 Deficit
This report shows a deficit of 
$16,198,000 for the first quarter of 
1922. This is at the rate of $64,000,000 
for the year.

For years the public has been given 
the impression that the post office de­
partment was self-sustaining or nearly 
so. Government ownership advocates 
continually use the federal postal sys­
tem as an efficient revenue-producing 
institution as an example which it 
would be well for the city of Chicago 
to follow.

The postal system has been repeat­
edly urged, locally and nationally, as 
a model of government operation on 
a profit-making basis.

The report made public by Gen. 
Dawes indicates that emphasis should 
be placed on “other causes” when the 
department—as on Dec. 3—ascribes 
the deficit for this year to “fire, burg­
lary, and other causes.”

Need for Publicity
It is only by statements of the char­
acter suppressed that Gen. Dawes be­
lieved government officials can know 
with certainty and accuracy how effi­
ciently and economically their various 
departments are being operated. He 
is a strong advocate of lower govern­
mental expenditures and cannot sit 
idly by while a method of reducing 
expense is pigeonholed. He would 
have the postal service on a self-sup­
porting basis, in fact as well as in 
reputation, or present the facts to the 
public.

The report and balance sheet were 
available last April, but Gen. Dawes 
said it was “chucked into the waste 
paper basket, so to speak, by a lot of 
subordinates who were opposed to the 
change and who, in his own organiza­
tion, had kept it from the attention 
of the controller general himself.”

Authors of Report
The report and balance sheet were 
prepared after six months of labor 
by F. C. Furlow, president of the 
Otis Elevator Company, at $1 per 
year, J. C. Knapp, vice president in 
charge of accounting, and four assist­
ants.

“The chances are,” said Gen. 
Dawes, “that no civilian organization 
could have employed these men at any 
price to do such work, while the gov­
ernment obtained it for $1 a year. 
If a private business organization 
could possibly have employed these 
men, it would not have paid less than 
$100,000 for the report.”

In starting the inquiry Gen. Dawes, 
as director of the budget, says that 
he had “the earnest co-operation of 
Postmaster General Hays”; but Hays’ 
successor, Postmaster General Work, 
has been quoted with reference to the

suppression of the balance sheet as 
saying: “It is a big fuss about noth­
ing.”

But Dawes insists that this impor­
tant work shall not be tossed aside so 
lightly. As the first director of the 
budget, he aided in reducing the ex­
pense of the federal government, as 
indicated by the following short tabu­
lation, showing the actual expense of 
the first two years and the estimated 
outlay for the last two:

This is an indicated reduction of 
more than 40 per cent, and the gen­
eral knows that part of it was inaug­
urated by the installation of “common 
sense statements.” His quarrel with 
Washington is because the statement 
plan prepared at his behest by officials 
of the Otis Elevator Company has 
been permitted to get dusty in the 
waste paper basket.

Gen. Dawes believes, because the 
Otis Elevator officials so reported to 
him after six months of investigation, 
that the accounts of the post office 
department “alone do not give a true 
picture.” The statements by the in­
vestigators add to the post office ac­
counts “what they lack” and “give a 
true picture,” in the opinion of the 
experts.

It is not work that kills men; it is 
worry. Work is healthy; you can 
hardly put more upon a man than he 
can bear. Worry is rust upon the 
blade. It is not the revolution that 
destroys the machinery, but the fric­
tion. Fear secretes acids, but love 
and trust are sweet juices.—Beecher.

Capitalism found a world of wood 
and iron ore and made it into a world 
of steel. How? It puddled the pig- 
iron until the dross was out and the 
pure metal was bessemered into steel. 
Now the task is to purify men as we 
have purified metals. Men have dross 
in their nature. They break under 
civilization’s load. A steel world is 
hopeless if men are pig-iron. There 
is greed and envy and malice in all 
of us. But also there is the real metal 
of brotherhood. Our task is to pud­
dle out the impurities so that the true 
iron can be strong enough to hold our 
civilization up forever. I have been 
a puddler of iron and I would be a 
puddler of men. Out of the best part 
of the iron I helped build a stronger 
world. Out of the best part of man’s 
metal let us build a better society.— 
Secretary of Labor Davis.

If I had a son, I would see that 
he would receive the highest educa­
tion in the United States. Such a 
type cannot be gained in universities 
and colleges, where class distinction 
and snobbishness is often too preva­
lent, although such institutions may 
give him supremacy in technical 
things.

But those traits do not make for 
a successful business man. The ideal 
education, to my mind, comes to the 
boy in the great public schools of this 
country. There is no real education 
except self-education. What he gets 
in school, after all, is only the founda­
tion on which his future success is 
built by actual effort.—Charles M. 
Schwab.

Accounting as an aid to good gov­
ernment is rapidly coming to the fore 
in the state and nation.

One middle western city is on the 
verge of an expose which threatens 
to rival the famous Tweed ring in 
New York. A demand has been made 
for an accounting of the expenditure 
of approximately one billion of dol­
lars in an administrative period of 
eight years.

In another part of the Forum this 
month will be found a story about 
the post office department’s balance 
sheet for the first quarter of 1922, 
which purports to show that the 
much-touted efficiency of the depart­

ment is a myth and the annual deficit 
amounts to a huge sum.

Public consciousness is awakening 
to the fact that local and national in­
efficiency and graft result primarily 
from the fact that good accounting is 
a negligible factor in the operation of 
many units which have an important 
bearing on the economic well-being of 
the individual citizen.

When politicians play fast and 
loose with the funds of a municipal­
ity and bureaucrats try to find ex­
cuses for spending all of an oversize 
appropriation, tax rates climb and 
misgovernment ensues, which could be 
prevented or lessened in some degree 
by provisions for an honest account­
ing of all expenditures. There is a 
growing demand for the economical 
operation of all municipal and gov­
ernmental agencies with the same 
check and net profit test that is ap­
plied to private business.

The old-fashioned method of elect­
ing an individual to an administrative 
position without regard to his special 
fitness or training has given way in 
many cities to the commission form 
of government, which has for its 
basis administration through budge­
tary control. Others have a business 
manager, instead of a mayor, who is 
selected because of his ability to run 
the departments in an efficient man­
ner, depending on his accountants to 
co-operate with him in keeping down 
expense and eliminating evils which 
creep in where political patronage 
burdens the pay rolls.

Good accounting will yet cure many 
evils which afflict the body politic.

Treasury Department Jobs
A story from The New York Times 
of November 7 gives some idea of 
the opportunities in the Income Tax 
Unit for accountants:

Confronted with the task of checking 
up on 25,000,000 income tax returns filed 
since 1917, the United States Treasury 
Department yesterday hung out the 
“Help Wanted” sign to catch the eyes 
of possible job hunters.

Salaries ranging from $1,800 to $3,000 
a year were the inducements offered. 
The qualifications were “at least the 
rudiments of theory and practice in ac­
counting.”

WASHINGTON, Nov. 6.—A special 
drive is being made by the Revenue 
Bureau to clean up pending 1917 tax 
cases involving many million dollars be­
fore they are outlawed under the statute 
of limitations next summer, it was said 
to-day at the Treasury Department.

Students of the I. A. S. course have 
been successful in practically every 
examination given for auditors in the 
unit since December, 1921.

Jot It Down
IF you’re going to meet a man, 
Jot it down.
If you’ve got a little plan, 
Jot it down.
If you never can remember
Your requirements for September 
’Till October or November, 
Jot ’em down.
IF you’ve got a note to pay, 
Jot it down.
If it’s due the first of May, 
Jot it down.
If collections are slow
That to meet the note you know
You must .dun Old Richard Roe, 
Jot it down.
IF you have a happy thought, 
Jot it down.
If there’s something to be bought, 
Jot it down.
Whether duty calls or pleasure, 
If you’re busy or at leisure, 
It will help you beyond measure, 
Jot it down.
IF there’re facts that you'd retain 
Jot ’em down.
If you’ve got to meet a train 
Jot it down.
If at work or only play,
If at home or far away, 
In the night or in the day, 
Jot it down.

At the year end business enjoyed a 
condition of prosperity even greater 
than had been anticipated at the out­
set. Expansion in the iron and steel 
industry, always a good barometer, 
was responsible in a great measure, 
and this is expected to continue well 
into 1923.

The only possible cloud on the do­
mestic horizon was the threat of la­
bor trouble if business continues good. 
The banking position was never bet­
ter in the history of the country, ex­
ports were improving, and good 
prices for crops increased the pur­
chasing power in various sections of 
the country.

During the past year confidence in 
the business and financial future of 
the country was re-established. The 
foreign situation, as well as the do­
mestic, showed an improvement from 
the standpoint of the United States, 
which year by year becomes a bigger 
factor in world markets.

Accounting Bill Delayed
The bill to create a board of ac­
countancy and regulate the practice 
of accountancy in the District lost its 
place on the unanimous consent calen­
dar of the House Dec. 5, when Repre­
sentative Thomas Blanton, of Texas, 
objected to its consideration.

Having lost its place on the unani­
mous consent calendar, the Zihlman 
accountancy bill can be brought before 
the House now only on District day. 
Scores of other District measures 
have been reported to the House by 
the District committee, and inasmuch 
as most of them have precedence over 
the accountancy bill, its chances of 
becoming law at this session of Con­
gress are not regarded as bright.

What Is Your Forecast?
F
OR 1923 John G. Shedd, chair­
man of the board of Marshall 
Field & Company, makes the follow­
ing prediction:

If our government exercises states­
manship, legislates wisely, and is guided 
by sound and successfully tried prin­
ciples, I can see prosperity advancing 
during 1923. Labor is fully employed 
at high wages. _ The farmer is now get­
ting better price for his crops. The 
country abounds in money—in fact, con­
ditions in this country are sound.

This forecast affects every ambi­
tious man or woman in business. 
Conditions are right for individual 
advancement. Better business brings 
more business. More business creates 
new firms and enlarges established 
firms. Opportunities to get ahead are 
in the making for you. Are you pre­
pared to take them?

Right now accounting knowledge is 
more valuable than ever before. 
Why? Because the recent depression 
has taught business heads the great 
value of determining and controlling 
their sales, their cost of production, 
and their overhead expense in mak­
ing net profit. This is done by cor­
rect and scientific interpretation of 
the accounting records. You can eas­
ily see the advantage held by men 
and women who have thoroughly 
mastered accounting.

Your business development for 
1923 depends on how hard you work 
and study.

Make a point of getting back to the 
lessons immediately, if you have 
dropped off during 1922. And re­
member that the successful I. A. S. 
student’s slogan is: “Two examina­
tions a week.”

Elmer L. Ludwig, 4722 Michigan 
Avenue, St. Louis, determined to get 
a better job where his I. A. S. train­
ing would be valuable. His success 
is related in the following letter, which 
was received by his instructors two 
days before Christmas:

From the interest you and your St. 
Louis Office have taken in me, I am 
sure you will be glad to learn of the 
good future that has come to me.

At the time of enrolling for the I. 
A. S. course I was employed by a large 
drug company and had gotten into a 
position offering no future. I gave the 
course the best I had, and after com­
pleting sixty-five lessons I made up my 
mind to look for something better. At 
the time of enrollment my salary was 
$110 per month.

At the suggestion of your St. Louis 
Office I addressed a letter to a number 
of firms asking for an interview and 
stating that in my spare time I had 
studied higher accounting and because 
of this fact was thoroughly qualified.

I sent out about thirty-five of these 
letters, selecting from the telephone di­
rectory such firms as I thought offered 
a good opportunity. I received several 
answers and have made a connection 
with a new firm as an assistant cost 
accountant and purchasing agent. This 
holds a much brighter future than ever 
before seemed possible.

I feel quite sure that the fact that I 
had made such good use of my spare 
time carried considerable weight in se­
curing the position, and I am absolutely 
sure that with my I. A. S. training I 
will be able to make good and advance 
rapidly with the growing and prosper­
ous company.

Mr. Ludwig has completed all of 
the basic section of the course and 
four lessons of the Systematizing and 
Cost Accounting group.

Accounting Training as a Part 
of Executive Equipment

On page two of this issue of the 
Forum there appears a statement by 
Charles E. Hires which reads in part, 
“A sound knowledge of general ac­
counting is essential in any business, 
to its successful and efficient adminis­
tration.”

When an executive so prominent 
as Mr. Hires makes a statement of 
this kind, his words carry weight, and 
when it is found that other executives 
of equal prominence not only believe 
the same thing but demonstrate its 
truth through the things they do, it 
is easy to understand why accounting 
training has gained prominence as a 
part of executive equipment.

Consequently, it may be no sur­
prise to the readers of the Forum to 
learn that Charles E. Mitchell, presi­
dent of the largest bank in the United 
States, The National City Bank of 
New York, in his early days added 
a knowledge of accounting to his 
business equipment.

Several years ago Mr. Mitchell 
went to work for the Western Elec­
tric Company in Chicago, in the order 
department, at a salary of $10 per 
week. And he was a college gradu­
ate, too—Amherst. He had been on 
the new job only a very short time 
when he discovered that, even though 
a college graduate, he did not know 
the meaning of the figures he handled 
every day.

“It was the sort of handicap,” ac­
cording to Bruce Barton in The 
American Magazine, “which many a 
college graduate would have taken 
lightly. He was in training for an 
executive office. Why should he 
bother with the details of bookkeep­
ing? He would be able later to hire 
men to attend to that. But he is the 
sort of man who is unhappy unless 
he knows all there is to know about 
the task he has in hand. At the end

of the first week, he put his ten dol­
lars in his pocket and hunted up a 
business college.”

Then he enrolled for a night course 
in bookkeeping which cost him $2 per 
week—a fifth of his weekly income, 
which left him very little for his liv­
ing expenses, clothes, and incidentals. 
But in six months he knew as much 
about bookkeeping as the other men 
with whom he worked—men who had 
obtained their knowledge by years of 
experience.

Equipped with this knowledge, Mr. 
Mitchell then conducted an investi­
gation of the various lines manufac­
tured by the company and soon dis­
covered that certain items were being 
manufactured and sold at a loss. This 
condition he set about to rectify and 
in working out his plans towards that 
end, he came in contact with a group 
of New York bankers which ulti­
mately led him to the presidency of 
the largest bank in the United States 
at the age of forty-five.

The number of men who have risen 
to the top of great enterprises 
through the accounting department, is 
really startling. There is not room 
here for all of them, but a few of the 
most prominent are: A. R. Erskine, 
president of the Studebaker Corpora­
tion of America; A. C. Bedford, 
chairman of the Standard Oil Com­
pany of New Jersey; John A. Top­
ping, chairman of the Republic Iron 
and Steel Company; Percy H. John­
ston, president of the Chemical Na­
tional Bank of New York; W. P. G. 
Harding, governor of the Federal 
Reserve Bank of Boston; and George 
M. Verity, president of the American 
Rolling Mill Company.

Certainly there is in the successes 
such men have wrought a powerful 
incentive for the I. A. S. student to 
make the most of his educational op­
portunities along accounting lines.

Every day I. A. S. students 
write their appreciation of the 
benefits of the course. A few let­
ters from men who have used the 
course to train for better posi­
tions and increases in salary are 
given here. On page 13 (oppo­
site) is reproduced a typical let­
ter from a progressive and sat­
isfied student.

New Positions
Mortimer D. Secord, Aurora, On­
tario, Canada, has completed sixty 
examinations and is now working on 
the Systematizing and Cost Account­
ing elective. Here is an excerpt from 
his letter of December 7, telling of 
a good position which resulted from 
his study of the course:

You will no doubt be pleased to know 
that, due to the knowledge gained from 
the course, I have been able to obtain 
an excellent position as accountant of 
a new manufacturing plant at a very 
good salary. Also, there is a very good 
opportunity for advancement.

Harold R. Kolseth, 728 North Ave­
nue, Baltimore, Md., placed this no­
tation on his sixteenth examination: 
This lesson comes in at a very op­
portune time as I have just received a 
position as bookkeeper due entirely to 
the training I have thus far received 
from the course. As it is just the end 
of the month, we got our statements 
from the bank, which I had to work 
up. I had just finished studying that 
portion of this lesson the previous eve­
ning; so it was very easy to do.”

A Better Position
J. L. Ford, P. O. Box 262, Smith­
ville, Tex., was unemployed for about 
four months, due to a reduction of 
the office force by the Missouri, Kan­
sas & Texas Railroad. He tells here 
how he was taken back on a better 
position:

I went back to work on a position 
above what I had before being let off. 
I want to say very frankly that it was 
all due to my training I received through 
your society.

Evening work brought some extra 
revenue to Robert Anderson, 663 East 
Maryland Street, St. Paul, Minn., 
after he had completed thirty exam­
inations. Here is his letter:

I am certainly proud to recommend 
your course to any friends that I have 
who I think would be interested in this 
kind of work. I am also glad to say 
that through your course I was able to 
run an advertisement in our local paper 
which secured for me an evening job 
taking care of a set of books, which I 
have just completed closing. This I 
would have been unable to do had I 
not completed a part of the course.

When C. T. Kohn, 1121 South El­
gin, Tulsa, Okla., lost his job, he kept 
on studying. His letter of December 
12 brought this good news:

Through the knowledge gained from 
the course I am now in a new position.

F. J. Bedner, 711 West Thirteenth 
Street, Chicago, graduated December 
21 and received his diploma in the 
nature of a Christmas present. He 
called on J. H. Millsaps, C. P. A., 
assistant educational director, and ex­
hibited an interesting chart of his 
progress. The following endorsement 
was written in a letter to the society:

Within two years after taking up the 
course my salary has increased more 
than 400 per cent, not mentioning how 
(Concluded on page 15)

International Accountants Society, Inc., 
2626 S. Michigan Are.,
Chicago, Ill.

I have Just been notified by our District Sales Manager 
that effective November 1st, 1922, I am to be Cashier and Office 
Manager of our branch house at Moline, Illinois.

This certainly came as a pleasant surprise to me, and I 
know you will be pleased, not only because I am one of your students 
but due to the fact that when I enrolled with your school, a little 
over one year ago, I was doing ordinary clerical work with not much 
of an outlook in my present organization.

I sincerely attribute a great share of my success so far 
to your wonderful school in Chicago, and also the encouragement and 
advice you have gladly given.

By George E. Bennett, C. P. A. Pub­
lished by McGraw-Hill Book Company, 
Inc., 370 Seventh Avenue, New York. 
661 pages.

Reviewed by Stephen Gilman, C. P. A.
T
HIS volume of 539 pages, which 
the author sets forth as a text­
book for classroom use rather than a 
reference volume for practicing ac­
countants, attempts to cover a field 
which has already been much better 
covered by other, authors.

The arrangement of the volume is 
bad from the teaching viewpoint. 
There seems to be little or no justifi­
cation for approaching the subject of 
advanced accounting with a discus­
sion of single versus double entry.

It would perhaps hardly be fair to 
expect to find anything new in ac­
counting technic or practice in a text­
book designed for classroom use. 
Textbooks frequently give the impres­
sion of having been built through the 
process of reading, combining, and re­
writing materials contained in all 
preceding texts on the same subject 
—a perfectly legitimate method but 
one which hardly results in a fresh 
viewpoint. Perhaps more than almost 
any other text this one by Mr. Ben­
nett gives this impression.

It seems to be the tendency of mod­
ern accounting authors to get away 
from a multiplicity of names for vari­
ous kinds and classes of accounts. 
It is to be regretted that Mr. Bennett 
did not follow this practice. On page 
eight he gives a list of 10 different 
classes of accounts which would prob­
ably serve to confuse the accounting 
student.

Occasionally through the reading 
matter one encounters positive state­

“Double entry requires more books 
than does single entry.”

Of course this statement may or 
may not be true. There are other au­
thors who call attention to single 
entry systems far more elaborate 
than the simplest double entry.

To sum up this criticism, Advanced 
Accounting appears to treat on all the 
various subjects which should be in­
cluded in such books, but without 
giving them that necessary structure 
or plan which has been found to be 
absolutely essential from the teach­
ing viewpoint.

By W. B. Reed, secretary of the Na­
tional Coal Association; consulting ac­
countant, Federal Taxation; formerly 
auditor, The New River Company, The 
White Oak Coal Company, ix, 221 pp. 
McGraw-Hill Book Company.
Reviewed by J. H. Millsaps, LL.B., C.P.A.

This publication grew out of some 
suggestions that were made by the 
National Coal Association with re­
gard to the determination of costs of 
producing coal. So many requests for 
these suggestions were received by the 
association that it was decided to pre­
pare a complete treatise on the sub­
ject of accounting for the bituminous 
industry.

All who have had any occasion to 
handle bituminous coal mine accounts 
will appreciate the need for such a 
publication. Heretofore help had to 
be secured from publications which 
dealt only incidentally with the sub­
ject. Some of the publications previ­
ously available dealt with mine ac-

counts in general or with coal mine 
costs, but there was nothing devoted 
exclusively to the specific problem of 
accounting for every phase of the bitu­
minous coal mining industry. Such is 
the aim of the book produced by Mr. 
Reed, and it must be said at the out­
set that the work has been well done.

Bituminous Coal Mine Accounting 
contains nineteen chapters dealing 
with many problems that must be 
solved by the accountant.

Some of the most difficult of these 
problems are accounting for supplies, 
power house fuel, royalties on leased 
properties, calculating depletion and 
depreciation charges, federal income 
tax, amortization of leasehold values, 
etc.

Probably the most useful feature of 
the book is the very full illustration of 
the many forms which are so essen­
tial in this industry. All the forms 
shown have been developed in actual 
work and will save much time for the 
accountant designing a system for a 
new development.

There is also an excellent discussion 
of the voucher system as applied to the 
bituminous mining industry. While 
the voucher system is usually regarded 
as a subject familiar to the students 
of accounting, its peculiar adaptability 
to coal mining gives it an appropriate 
place in a treatise of this kind.

The thoroughness with which the 
author has covered the subject and the 
minuteness of detail with which he 
has dealt with its peculiar problems 
make this an especially valuable con­
tribution to accounting literature.

Millionaire (speaking to body of 
students)—“All my success, all my 
tremendous financial prestige, I owe 
to one thing alone—pluck, pluck, 
pluck.”

Student—“But how are we to find 
the right people to pluck?”—Dinuba 
Sentinel.

(Concluded from page 12) 
it brightened my future walk of life. 
The society can always consider me as 
one of its staunchest supporters.

F. O. Wilie, 67 Thorpe Street, Pon­
tiac, Mich., an I. A. S. student and 
enthusiast, has been promoted. Here 
is a part of his letter:

When I started taking this course, I 
was only one of the cogs in the ac­
counts payable at the Oakland Motor 
Car Company’s plant. Due to your good 
course I have been placed in charge.

Mr. Wilie has completed forty­
eight examinations.

W. N. Waller, Jr., 619 Redgate 
Avenue, Norfolk, Va., thanked his C. 
P. A. instructors and told of a pro­
motion as the result of his training:

At this time I would like to thank 
the members of the staff for the bene­
fits I have received from the course. 
From a financial point I have already 
received an increase of one-third in nine 
months, and from a point of promotion 
I am doing a little work by myself, 
whereas I started as a junior last Janu­
ary.

Mr. Waller has completed forty- 
six examinations.

Fifty Feet from Success
When the Farmers Bank of Canada 
failed, there was a scandal. One man 
was sent to prison, and others were 
disgraced because the funds of the 
depositors had been used in the Keeley 
Silver Mine project, in which various 
of the officials were interested.

After the fall of the bank an Eng­
lish concern took over the Keeley 
mine. Fifty feet beyond that reached 
by the drills of the banking syndicate 
the English struck a vein of silver 
which is said to yield 2,000 ounces 
to the ton.

Those Farmers Bank men now can 
understand what was meant by being 
in the right church but the wrong pew.

One of the marked advantages of 
machine posting is the continuous 
proof of balance, the machine auto­
matically accumulating the charges or 
credits as posted and giving the total 
at the end of the run of the day, 
which can be compared with the pre­
viously established control. Should 
there be an error, it can and must be 
located and corrected before leaving 
this section of the work. Machine- 
kept accounts are interpreted at a 
glance because they are clearly printed 
and perfectly aligned, and kept in this 
way are so easily understood that 
some credit checkers are able to 
handle one or two hundred orders an 
hour. By the use of a sheet of carbon 
paper the monthly statement can be 
made at the time the customer’s ac­
count is posted and be ready to put 
into the mail as soon as the final 
charges for the month have been 
posted.

It is estimated that about 30 per 
cent card space is saved by machines 
as compared with hand posting.—The 
Credit Monthly.

Pessimism
The man who does not hope for 
better things and does not believe that 
better things can be brought about is 
not the man likely to bring better 
things about. Pessimism is produc­
tive of paralysis and stagnation.—W. 
Taft.

In 1920 the people of the United 
States spent $22,700,000,000 on luxu­
ries—an average of $200 apiece for 
every man, woman, and child. This 
was fully a third more than the 
amount expended altogether for edu­
cation.

Business Is Business* 
“Business is Business,” the Little Man 
said, 
“A battle where ‘everything goes,’ 
Where the only gospel is ‘get ahead,’ 
And never spare friends or foes;
‘Slay or be slain,’ is the slogan cold, 
You must struggle and slash and tear, 
For Business is Business, a fight for gold, 
Where all that you do is fair!”

“Business is Business,” the Big Man said, 
“A battle to make of earth
A place to yield us more wine and bread 
More pleasure and joy and mirth;
There are still some bandits and buc­
caneers
Who are jungle-bred beasts of trade, 
But their number dwindles with passing 
years
And dead is the code they made!”

"And those who make it a ruthless fight 
Have only themselves to blame
If they feel no whit of the keen delight 
In playing the Bigger Game, 
The game that calls on the heart and 
head,
The best of man’s strength and nerve: 
“Business is Business,” the Big Man said, 
“And that Business is to serve!"
—Berton Braley.

Improves Office Methods
Income tax reports are not so dif­
ficult for P. L. Shafer, of the Kis- 
sell Companies, Springfield, Ohio, 
now that he has completed the I. A. 
S. course. In a recent letter he says:

I have completely reclassified the ac­
counts of all our corporations according 
to the methods taught and recommended 
by the Society, thereby making a very 
substantial reduction in the volume of 
detail work involved and greatly in­
creasing the efficiency of. my department. 
The theories and principles which I 
have developed in this classification have 
been worked out to conform to the re­
quirements of the federal income tax 
laws as affecting the real estate business, 
thereby eliminating all the worry and 
hard work incident to the filing of these 
reports.
Sixteen

Scientific, Direct Methods for
Knowing the Meaning of Figures

How to determine 
the Facts that lead to 
greater net profits — 
how to search for the 
correct answers to the 
problems of sales, pur­
chases, labor, expense, 
and finance, as reflected 
by the accounting sys­
tem. Ten loose-leaf les­
sons, numerous charts 
and diagrams dealing 
strictly with simple 
statistics, as applied to 
current business prob­
lems.

Prepared from the 
actual experience of ac­
tively practicing ac­
countants. All study di­
rected by experienced

accountant instructors. 
Every one a C. P. A. 
by examination.

This course is an 
elective of our complete 
course in Accounting, 
offered separately to 
those whose previous 
training and experience 
qualifies them for 
‘‘post - graduate’’ in­
struction.

If you are one of 
these, send for our 
descriptive pamphlet. 
Our general course pre­
pares thoroughly for 
both private and public 
accounting work.

“The Professional School of Accounting”
2626 South Michigan Avenue, Chicago, Illinois

New, vital facts presented for the first 
time in a complete course fitting 
accountants of advanced standing for 
the position of Chief Accountant

To meet the growing demand 
for executive and chief account­
ants this remarkable, practical 
extension course has been pre­
pared. Every duty, oppor­
tunity, and function of the 
Comptroller, Auditor, and 
Chief Accountant is analyzed, 
classified, and clearly set forth 
by thoroughly competent, prac­
tical, experienced accountant­
instructors, with individual at­
tention to the work of each 
student.
Ten lessons in looseleaf form, 
perpetually revised. A num­

ber of original charts and 
graphs give a volume of in­
struction in compact, compre­
hensible, usable form.

To those who are fitted by ex­
perience and training, we will 
gladly send this valuable book­
let. Full of facts and interest­
ing, authentic information 
about Internal Auditing. A 
complete summary of our spe­
cial course.

“The Professional School of Accounting”
2626 South Michigan Avenue, Chicago, Illinois
